steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The blank follows "well ___" and must correctly separate the first independent clause ("...but many are still well ___") from the second independent clause ("Malika Ouattara, for example, is a celebrated slam poet from Burkina Faso"). A colon can separate two independent clauses when the second one elaborates on or gives an example of the first.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'known': leaves no punctuation between two independent clauses, creating a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — 'known,': a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — this is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'known:': a colon correctly separates two independent clauses when the second illustrates or elaborates on the first. Malika Ouattara is an example of a writer who is still well known, so the colon is appropriate here. ✓
  • Choice D — 'known and': "and" is a coordinating conjunction that joins two grammatically parallel elements. It works between two independent clauses only without a comma before it if the clauses are short, but here it creates an awkward, illogical join that loses the example relationship signaled by "for example" in the second clause. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
